metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit E2E staging scenario to AC line 6 (or a new operator-attested AC) where the env-preflight guard **specifically fires**: clear required-reviewers on the staging fork's `pypi` environment, run dispatch-from-main, and verify the release aborts before the publish job — attested by the staging run URL.
findings.finding: The env-preflight step — described as 'the single most security-critical gate' — is validated only through canned fixture JSON in unit tests (parser logic), and the operator-attested E2E staging run does NOT enumerate a scenario where this guard specifically fires (i.e., env-protection absent → release aborts). This is a proxy-validation gap: the live API call and the fail-closed abort decision are never exercised end-to-end in any testable artifact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — unit test AC (AC line 5) and operator-attested E2E AC (AC line 6); 'What' section — 'Human authorization' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC line 6 to include: 'a staging run where the pypi environment has required-reviewers cleared aborts at the preflight step (staging run URL attested).' This makes the fail-closed behavior of the live API call verifiable end-to-end, not merely as a parser fixture.

findings.finding: The preflight step's failure path — the release aborting when the `pypi` environment lacks required reviewers or has a non-`main`-only deployment-branch policy — is a new security-critical control flow that is never exercised in the stated testing plan. AC 5's three operator-attested staging scenarios cover non-`main` ref, version mismatch, and non-ancestor HEAD, but not the case where the preflight API call detects a misconfigured environment and fails closed. This is the highest-leverage guard in the plan ("the single most security-critical gate") yet its failure mode has no corresponding test attestation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 (preflight step) and AC 5 (operator-attested guard-firing scenarios)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 5 with: 'and a staging `pypi` environment temporarily missing required reviewers causes the preflight step to fail and abort the release before build' — exercised in the `navapbc/rebar-staging` fork against a TestPyPI environment configured without required reviewers, producing an attested failing run URL.

findings.finding: The described work in Scope explicitly states this story 'rewrites the now-obsolete existing sections it invalidates — the "Tag → PyPI publishes automatically" procedure and the "Hard rules" manual-lockstep framing', but AC #7 only verifies that `docs/releasing.md` *adds* new content ('documents the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement'). It does not verify removal of the old tag-based procedure and manual-lockstep sections. A partial doc update that retains the contradictory old procedure would pass AC #7 as written.
findings.location: Scope — `docs/releasing.md` entry; AC #7
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC #7 as: '`docs/releasing.md` removes (or clearly marks as superseded) the "Tag → PyPI publishes automatically" procedure and the "Hard rules" manual-lockstep section, AND documents the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the preflight step's parsing logic by citing the exact GitHub REST API response fields (`protection_rules[].type`, `deployment_branch_policy.protected_branches` vs `deployment_branch_policy.custom_branch_policies`) and add a unit-test fixture for each `deployment_branch_policy` mode (both `protected_branches: true` and `custom_branch_policies: true`/`false`) to ensure the parser handles both shapes.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a preflight step that calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` and parses the response to detect 'required reviewers absent' and 'deployment-branch policy not main-only', but never names or verifies the GitHub REST API response schema fields it will parse — a non-trivial assertion given that the `deployment_branch_policy` object has two distinct modes (`protected_branches` vs. `custom_branch_policies`) with different JSON structures.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC line 4 ('A release-time preflight step calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the exact `permissions:` block required for the preflight job (e.g., `contents: read` or the specific `environments: read` grant if available) in the AC or workflow spec, and specify that any non-200 response from `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` is treated as a hard failure (fail-closed), so a 404-from-missing-scope cannot be silently confused with a 200-environment-absent response.
findings.finding: The preflight step that calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` does not declare the required `permissions:` grant for the workflow job, nor specify how non-200 responses (e.g., a 404 from insufficient token scope rather than a genuinely absent environment) are distinguished from a true 'environment not configured' response — leaving the fail-closed security guarantee on this boundary-crossing path with an undeclared and potentially ambiguous access contract.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC line: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and fails the release closed…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the relevant AC or Scope bullet: 'The preflight job declares `permissions: contents: read` (or the minimum grant that allows the environments API call) and its error-handling treats any HTTP status other than 200 as an abort — a non-200 that could indicate a scope/permissions failure is logged with the raw status code and fails the step, ensuring a token-scope regression does not silently disable the reviewer gate.'

findings.finding: The preflight step asserts it can determine 'required reviewers absent' and 'deployment-branch policy is not main-only' by calling `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`. This is an unverified capability assertion about the GitHub Environments REST API response shape. The GitHub API returns environment protection rules on this endpoint, but the specific field paths for required-reviewer presence and the deployment-branch-policy 'main-only' condition are not cited. A wrong field path would cause the preflight to silently pass (treating a missing field as 'protection present') — the opposite of the stated fail-closed intent.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not `main`-only'; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the preflight step design, reference https://docs.github.com/en/rest/deployments/environments#get-an-environment and specify: check `protection_rules` array for an entry with `type == 'required_reviewers'` and non-empty `reviewers`; check `deployment_branch_policy.protected_branches == true` (or the equivalent custom-branch-policy field). Add a test in the staging run (AC5) that deliberately clears the environment protection and confirms the preflight exits non-zero.

findings.finding: The preflight step's GitHub API permission requirement is asserted but not specified: the plan states the step 'needs only the workflow's own read token' to call GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and inspect protection_rules (required reviewers + deployment-branch policy), but the exact GITHUB_TOKEN permission scope needed for that endpoint is unstated. If the workflow's token lacks the necessary permission (e.g. environments: read is required in fine-grained tokens, or admin access is needed for protection_rules on private repos), the preflight silently returns an empty or truncated response and the fail-closed guarantee is lost.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment + a fail-closed preflight' bullet; Scope section first bullet; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add `environments: read` to the job's `permissions:` block for the preflight step, and note in the plan that this permission is required for the `protection_rules` field to be populated in the API response. Verify with: `curl -H 'Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN' https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi | jq '.protection_rules'` in a test run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the env-preflight step treats any non-200 HTTP response (timeout, 5xx, 429) as a hard failure (fail-closed), and document this behavior in the AC or the step's inline comment so the implementation cannot accidentally fail-open on transient API errors.
findings.finding: The preflight step that calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` does not specify its failure mode on transient API errors (timeout, 5xx, rate-limit, GitHub outage). The plan says the step 'FAILS the release closed' when protection is *absent*, but is silent on what happens when the API call itself fails — a fail-open implementation would silently skip the gate and permit a single-actor publish during a GitHub API outage.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight' and AC line: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/... and fails the release closed when required reviewers are absent'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the preflight AC: 'Any non-200 response from the GitHub API (including timeout and rate-limit) aborts the release with an explicit error; the step never skips the check on transport failure.' In the workflow step itself, use `|| { echo 'env-preflight API error — aborting'; exit 1; }` after the curl/gh call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and unit-test the env-preflight step's behavior under GitHub API errors: add a fixture for HTTP non-200 (e.g., 403, 500) and malformed/empty JSON responses, and document whether the step fails-closed (blocks release) or retries on transient errors.
findings.finding: The env-preflight step's behavior under GitHub API errors (HTTP 4xx, 5xx, timeout, or malformed/empty response) is not addressed by any test or AC. If the API call fails for reasons unrelated to missing protection (e.g., transient 500, token scope issue), the step could fail-open (allowing publish) or produce a confusing hard-fail that blocks legitimate releases — neither outcome is specified or tested.
findings.location: 'What' section — 'Human authorization / preflight step' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add two additional unit-test fixtures to the env-preflight test: one where the API returns a non-200 status (assert the step fails closed) and one where the response body is malformed JSON (assert fail closed). Document the intended behavior in the preflight step's inline comment or the releasing.md update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the 'second human approves each publish' invariant is mechanically enforced against self-approval — either document that GitHub prevents self-approval in this context (verify against GitHub's deployment review docs), require >= 2 reviewers in the preflight check, or mandate a team-reviewer pattern where the dispatcher cannot be the sole approving member.
findings.finding: The plan's 'single most security-critical gate' — the pypi GitHub environment's required-reviewers setting — does not mechanically enforce 'a second human approves each publish' because GitHub deployment environment reviews, unlike PR reviews, permit the workflow dispatcher to self-approve their own review. If the person who dispatches the workflow is also listed as a required reviewer on the pypi environment, they can unilaterally approve and publish with no second human involved. The plan never specifies that the reviewer list must exclude the dispatcher, or that a team-reviewer pattern should be used so the dispatcher cannot be the sole approving member.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet; AC line 4 (preflight step AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a preflight check assertion: `required_reviewers >= 2` (so one self-approval is insufficient), OR specify that the pypi environment uses a team reviewer (e.g., a 'release-approvers' team) that excludes the dispatcher by org policy, and document this constraint in the AC and the preflight step specification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the preflight step specification to explicitly require fail-closed behavior on ALL non-200 responses from the GitHub environments API (including 5xx, 429, network errors), not only on the policy-absent case — so transient API unavailability blocks the release rather than silently permitting it.
findings.finding: The preflight step's fail-closed property is specified only for the policy-configuration-absent case, not for GitHub REST API errors (5xx, timeout, rate-limit). The plan says the step 'FAILS the release closed' if required_reviewers are absent or the branch policy is not main-only, but does not specify that a transient API error must also fail closed. An implementer following the plan literally could implement fail-open on API errors (e.g., treat a timeout as 'assume OK and proceed'), which would create a bypass: if the GitHub API is unreachable at release time, the preflight passes silently and a misconfigured environment could publish.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet; Scope section — preflight step specification; AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the preflight step spec: 'treat any non-200 HTTP response from the environments API as a failure — exit non-zero, block the release. Only a 200 response with the correct policy shape should allow the release to proceed.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that the preflight step 'fails the release closed' when the environment configuration is wrong, but does not specify behavior when the GitHub REST API call itself fails (network timeout, 5xx error, auth/rate-limit error). In a production security gate, an unhandled API-call failure that silently exits zero or is caught as 'no reviewers found' could open a publish path where protection was intended.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet; Scope section — '.github/workflows/release.yml preflight step'; AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the preflight AC or the 'Human authorization' bullet: 'Any non-200 response, network failure, or schema parse error from the environments API also fails the release closed (the step uses `set -e` or equivalent and treats an absent/malformed response identically to a missing-reviewer response).'

findings.finding: The preflight step's decision protocol has no specified fallback for sub-step failure: the plan says the step 'FAILS the release closed' when the API response indicates misconfiguration (missing reviewers, wrong branch policy), but never states what should happen when the GitHub REST API call itself fails — network timeout, 5xx error, auth/rate-limit error, or unexpected response schema. An API failure that exits non-zero generically (not as a 'release closed' signal) or that is swallowed and exits zero would produce undefined behavior at the most security-critical gate in the plan.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight' bullet; Scope — '.github/workflows/release.yml preflight step'; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the preflight bullet: 'The step treats any non-200 response, network error, or JSON parse failure as a hard failure (exit non-zero) — the preflight is fail-closed by construction, not only on detected misconfiguration. The step's shell/script logic must not swallow errors (use `set -euo pipefail` and validate the HTTP status code before parsing the body).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling for the `GET /environments/pypi` preflight: define a timeout, cap retries (e.g. 1 retry with short backoff), distinguish 4xx/5xx from a policy-absent 200, and surface a human-readable error message that identifies which condition fired.
findings.finding: The preflight call to `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` has no specified error-handling for API unavailability (timeout, 5xx, rate-limit) vs. a policy-absent 200 response. The plan says 'fails the release closed' but does not distinguish these cases, so an operator cannot tell from logs/output whether the gate fired due to a flaky GitHub API or a genuine misconfiguration — and there is no mention of timeout, retry ceiling, or surfaced error message.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; Scope section; AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: In the preflight step, use `--fail-with-body --max-time 10 --retry 1` with `curl`/`gh api`, then branch on HTTP status: non-200 → emit 'GitHub API unavailable — release aborted (transient)' and exit 1; 200 with missing reviewers → emit 'pypi environment lacks required reviewers — release aborted (policy gap)' and exit 1. This makes the two failure modes distinguishable in logs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a named section in CLAUDE.md / docs/architecture/) capturing: the threat model (arbitrary tag → reviewed-main dispatch), the rejected alternatives (tag-lock in Terraform, terraform-drift coupling for the preflight), and the invariants that must be preserved (environment must have required reviewers + main-only policy; tag is an output never a trigger) — referenced from docs/releasing.md.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel, security-critical architectural decision — replacing tag-based release authorization with dispatch-from-main, a fail-closed preflight (over terraform coupling), and OIDC trusted publishing as the exclusive release path — but scopes only an operational update to docs/releasing.md with no ADR or equivalent durable design record capturing the threat model, the rejected alternatives (tag-lock in terraform, terraform-drift coupling), and the security invariants future maintainers must preserve.
findings.location: Scope section ('docs/releasing.md') and 'What' section ('main.tf / tag-push restriction stays OUT of scope', 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment')
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0001-dispatch-from-main-release-authorization.md` (or equivalent) covering: context (tag-push authorization gap), decision (workflow_dispatch from main + preflight + OIDC), rejected alternatives (terraform tag-lock, terraform-drift coupling), and consequences (environment re-key, tag-as-output invariant). Add a one-line reference to it in docs/releasing.md. Include this file in the Scope section's deliverables and add an AC: 'An ADR documenting the release-authorization threat model and rejected alternatives exists in the repository — codebase-verifiable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add idempotency handling for the post-publish tag and GitHub Release creation steps: check whether the tag/release already exists before creating (e.g. `gh release view v$VERSION` exit-code guard) so a re-run after partial failure can complete safely without re-attempting the PyPI upload.
findings.finding: If the workflow succeeds at `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish` (package is live on PyPI) but then fails during the subsequent tag-creation or GitHub Release step, a re-run of the dispatch will attempt to publish the same version again and be rejected by PyPI's duplicate-version check — leaving the release in a permanently broken state with no specified recovery path.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish (record only)'; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Split the publish and post-publish steps into separate jobs. In the tag/release job, guard creation with `gh release view "v$VERSION" 2>/dev/null || gh release create "v$VERSION" ...` so re-runs are idempotent. Document this re-run procedure in `docs/releasing.md` under a 'Recovering from a partial release' subsection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` lines 6-8 to replace the 'tags MUST stay human-pushable so release.yml can publish on a v* tag push' rationale with the new invariant ('tags stay open as record-only output of dispatch-from-main; publication safety now comes from the re-keyed pypi environment, not tag access control').
findings.finding: The plan declares `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` and its no-tag-lock decision 'remain unchanged', but the test's embedded rationale comment (line 6) explicitly states 'tags MUST stay human-pushable so `.github/workflows/release.yml` can publish to PyPI on a `v*` tag push' — a rationale that this plan directly invalidates by removing the `push: tags: v*` publish trigger. This comment is a codebase-verifiable false statement after the plan lands and represents an unenumerated consumer update.
findings.location: What section / 'main.tf / tag-push restriction stays OUT of scope' bullet; tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py line 6
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_mirror_lock_terraform.py`, update the module docstring bullet and the inline comment on line 42 from the old rationale ('tags stay human-pushable so release.yml v* pushes publish to PyPI') to the new one ('tags are created post-publish as record only; the no-tag-lock decision remains, but publication safety is now dispatch-from-main + the re-keyed pypi environment, not tag access control').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default implementation technology for the extracted guard helpers (e.g., 'Python functions in `scripts/release_guards.py`, called from workflow shell steps via `python scripts/release_guards.py`; unit-tested in `tests/unit/test_release_guards.py` and covered by `make test`') so the executor has a concrete target.
findings.finding: The implementation technology/mechanism for the 'extracted guard helpers' (version-lockstep comparison, ancestry check, env-preflight response parsing) is never stated. The plan says 'extract… into small testable units' but leaves the executor to choose between Python modules, shell scripts, a dedicated scripts/ file, or inline bash — directly affecting where the test files live, how they're invoked by `make test`, and how the workflow YAML calls them. This is a 'choose an appropriate X' placeholder deferred to the executor.
findings.location: What section — 'Verify the guard LOGIC with unit tests' bullet; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Verify the guard LOGIC with unit tests' bullet: 'Implement each guard as a Python function in `scripts/release_guards.py` (callable from workflow `run:` steps via `python scripts/release_guards.py <subcommand>`); unit tests live in `tests/unit/test_release_guards.py` and are exercised by `make test`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prerequisite AC (or a preflight step in AC2) that asserts F3's and F4's jobs are present in `release.yml` before verifying they are retained — e.g., 'verify `release.yml` contains a `test-wheel` job and an `mcp_verify` job (as evidence F3+F4 have landed) before checking that this story's changes do not remove them'.
findings.finding: AC2 asserts the workflow 'retains F3's build-once → test-wheel/test-sdist → hash-gated-publish job graph and F4's mcp_verify/mcp_registry split', but no acceptance criterion expresses the prerequisite that F3 and F4 must already be merged. If this story is merged before F3/F4, AC2 is unprovable (those jobs simply do not yet exist). The dependency is stated only in prose ('this story lands last', 'F4 → F3 → F1'), not as a gate that blocks the AC from being checked.
findings.location: AC2; 'Compose with — do not regress — F3 and F4' What section bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Prepend to AC2: 'Prerequisite: `release.yml` already contains a `test-wheel`/`test-sdist` job (F3) and an `mcp_verify` job (F4); if absent, this AC is BLOCKED, not passing.' This makes the dependency machine-checkable rather than prose-only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item (or extend AC item 2) that explicitly verifies the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes are recorded in the release evidence artifact — codebase-verifiable from the workflow definition's evidence-recording step.
findings.finding: The 'What' section includes a standalone deliverable — 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence' — that has no corresponding acceptance criterion in the AC list, making it an orphan described work item.
findings.location: 'What' section, final bullet: 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence'; AC list (no matching item)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC item 2 with a trailing clause: '...and the evidence artifact captures the dispatch SHA, input version, all manifest versions asserted by the lockstep step, and artifact hashes — codebase-verifiable from the workflow definition.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out procedure to the plan (and to docs/releasing.md): e.g., a documented 'emergency revert' commit that re-adds the tag trigger and re-keys the environment back, or an expand-contract phase where the dispatch path is proven on the staging fork before the tag trigger is removed from the production workflow.
findings.finding: The plan removes the push:tags:v* publish trigger — an irreversible behavioral change to the release workflow — and explicitly justifies it, but provides no rollback or back-out path if the new dispatch-from-main flow is discovered to be broken after the story lands in the production workflow.
findings.location: What section ('No raw-tag publish path'); Scope ('.github/workflows/release.yml — Remove the push: tags: v* publish trigger'); AC item 1 ('no push: tags publish trigger')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Rollback section to the Scope or docs/releasing.md: 'If the dispatch flow is found broken post-landing, the back-out is: (1) revert the release.yml commit via Gerrit to restore the push:tags trigger, (2) temporarily re-key the pypi environment to accept v* tags, (3) diagnose and re-land the fix.' This makes the reversibility mechanism explicit rather than implicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the release-evidence lifecycle: specify where evidence is stored (e.g. as a GitHub Actions job summary + a GitHub Release asset), which step/job creates it, who/what consumes it (e.g. the `mcp_registry` job or a post-release audit step), and whether a retention policy applies.
findings.finding: The plan mentions 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence' but does not specify the storage medium (job summary, artifact upload, GitHub Release asset, a file committed to the repo), who owns/consumes this evidence, or how it is retired/cleaned up — leaving the shared-state lifecycle undefined.
findings.location: What section — last bullet of 'What'; Scope section (not mentioned explicitly)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Codify the `pypi` environment's required-reviewer list and `main`-only deployment-branch policy as a Terraform resource (e.g., `github_repository_environment` + `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy`) so the gate is reproducible as-code and drift is restorable, not merely detectable; or explicitly justify why the preflight-only approach is an accepted residual risk and document the manual re-creation runbook.
findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub environment re-key (required reviewers + `main`-only deployment-branch policy) is explicitly kept OUT of Terraform/IaC and is operator-attested via console — making the single most security-critical gate an un-versioned, out-of-band configuration that is not reproducible as-code. The preflight step is detective-only (fires at release time) but cannot restore the policy if it is silently cleared between releases, and no IaC resource codifies or enforces the desired state.
findings.location: Scope section — '`infra/terraform-github/main.tf` and its no-tag-lock contract test remain **unchanged**'; What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment'; AC line '[operator-attested] The live `pypi` GitHub environment matches the codified terraform…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `github_repository_environment` resource block to `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` for the `pypi` environment, setting `reviewers` (user/team IDs) and a deployment branch policy restricted to `main`. Add a corresponding contract test (`test_pypi_environment_has_required_reviewers`) alongside the existing `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock`. The preflight step remains as defense-in-depth. If the AWS-vs-GitHub auth split in `terraform-drift.yml` is truly blocking, note that the `github_repository_environment` resource can be managed in a separate, GitHub-authed Terraform workspace — but do not leave the configuration uncodified.

findings.finding: The staging/TestPyPI dry-run required by AC5 depends on the navapbc/rebar-staging fork's release.yml being configured for TestPyPI OIDC trusted publishing ('TestPyPI project nava-rebar (test.pypi.org) via TestPyPI OIDC trusted publishing'), but this setup work — configuring the fork's workflow and registering the OIDC trusted publisher on test.pypi.org — is absent from the Scope section, leaving the executor unclear whether it is pre-existing infrastructure they can rely on or work they must complete as part of this story.
findings.location: What section — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves' bullet; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope bullet: 'navapbc/rebar-staging fork — confirm (or establish) that the fork's release.yml is configured to publish to TestPyPI project nava-rebar via OIDC trusted publishing; if not already set up, create the TestPyPI trusted-publisher entry (owner: navapbc, repo: rebar-staging, workflow: release.yml, env: testpypi) as a prerequisite to AC5 attestation.'

findings.finding: The 'What' section describes the staging fork (`navapbc/rebar-staging`) as needing a `release.yml` that publishes to TestPyPI via OIDC trusted publishing, implying setup work in that fork. This is described as part of the plan's testing strategy but is absent from the Scope section and not verifiably covered by any AC — AC #5 only operator-attests that guards fire, not that the staging fork is correctly configured. The staging fork setup is a prerequisite for the guard-firing tests but has no deliverable or criterion.
findings.location: What — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves' section; Scope section; AC #5

findings.finding: The plan designates `navapbc/rebar-staging` as the 'default' home for dry-run / negative-path testing and asserts its `release.yml` 'publishes to the TestPyPI project `nava-rebar` (test.pypi.org) via TestPyPI OIDC trusted publishing' — but there is zero codebase evidence that this fork exists, that TestPyPI OIDC trusted publishing is configured for it, or that it has a `release.yml` at all. No file in the repo references `rebar-staging`.
findings.location: What section — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves'; AC5 '[operator-attested] A staging/TestPyPI dry run demonstrates the guards fire'

findings.finding: The plan introduces `navapbc/rebar-staging` + a TestPyPI trusted-publishing registration as a new external dependency to host negative-path dry runs, but this infrastructure has zero prior presence in the codebase and the plan does not verify or document its setup requirements (fork creation, TestPyPI OIDC trusted-publisher registration for the fork's `release.yml`, `nava-rebar` project on test.pypi.org). This is a novel architectural addition — a dedicated staging repo for CI testing — that the plan presents as 'default' without comparing it to simpler alternatives that avoid the external dependency.
findings.location: What section — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves' bullet: 'dry runs relocate to the `navapbc/rebar-staging` fork (default), whose `release.yml` publishes to the TestPyPI project `nava-rebar` (test.pypi.org) via TestPyPI OIDC trusted publishing'

findings.finding: The plan relocates dry-run and negative-path testing to `navapbc/rebar-staging` with 'its `release.yml` publishes to the TestPyPI project `nava-rebar` (test.pypi.org) via TestPyPI OIDC trusted publishing.' TestPyPI OIDC trusted publishing requires a separately registered pending publisher on test.pypi.org (project name, org, repo, workflow filename, environment) distinct from the existing PyPI registration. The plan provides no setup steps for this registration, no confirmation the `nava-rebar` project exists on test.pypi.org, and no evidence the `navapbc/rebar-staging` fork exists. AC5 (operator-attested guard-firing runs) depends on this infrastructure being functional, making it a prerequisite that is entirely out-of-scope and unverified.
findings.location: What section — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves'; AC5

findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a required staging/TestPyPI fork (`<org>/rebar-staging` fork with its own `release.yml` publishing to TestPyPI `nava-rebar`) as necessary for negative testing, but this fork's setup and configuration are not listed as a Scope deliverable, and AC6 (operator-attested) only requires 'staging run URLs' without specifying that the fork must be set up. An implementer has no codebase deliverable to produce for the staging fork.
findings.location: What section ('dry runs relocate to a named staging target: a <org>/rebar-staging fork') vs. Scope section (no entry for staging fork) vs. AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'no terraform changes in scope' (Scope) and 'attested by terraform plan showing no drift' (last operator-attested AC): either (a) bring the pypi-environment terraform resource into scope for this story so the plan reflects the re-key, or (b) rewrite the AC verification condition to rely solely on the org-admin/API confirmation path and drop the terraform-plan reference.
findings.finding: The environment re-key is a deliverable of this story and terraform changes are explicitly out of scope, yet the last [operator-attested] AC item verifies the live environment configuration by 'a terraform plan showing no drift' — if the terraform resource for the pypi environment is not updated to reflect the re-keyed branch policy, a terraform plan would show drift rather than confirm absence of it, making the verification condition self-defeating.
findings.location: Scope section (last bullet: 'No infra/terraform-github/... changes'); AC last [operator-attested] item: 'attested by an terraform plan showing no drift or an org-admin confirmation'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the last [operator-attested] AC item's verification condition with: 'attested by an org-admin or the release-time preflight output confirming required reviewers are enabled and the deployment-branch policy is main-only.' Remove the terraform-plan clause, since terraform is explicitly out of scope. Separately, note in Scope that updating the terraform resource to codify the new branch policy is a follow-on task.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm and document that the existing PyPI pending publisher registration (`workflow: release.yml`, `environment: pypi`) requires no changes when the trigger switches from `push: tags: v*` to `workflow_dispatch` on `main`, citing PyPI's OIDC claim-matching rules (repo + workflow + environment, not ref/trigger) so operators do not incorrectly recreate the registration.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that PyPA Trusted Publishing (OIDC) will work when the workflow trigger changes from `push: tags: v*` to `workflow_dispatch` on `main`, treating the existing pending publisher registration (registered with `environment: pypi`) as sufficient — but never explicitly confirms that PyPI's OIDC subject-claim validation accepts the changed trigger context.
findings.location: What section — 'Build, then publish DIRECTLY via PyPA Trusted Publishing'; Scope — '.github/workflows/release.yml'; AC line 3 ('publishes directly via the OIDC trusted-publishing flow')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope or docs/releasing.md section: 'PyPI Trusted Publishing matches on repo + workflow filename + environment name — it does NOT filter by the triggering ref or event type, so the existing `nava-rebar` pending publisher registration requires no update (confirmed: https://docs.pypi.org/trusted-publishers/using-a-publisher/).' Include this in the docs/releasing.md update AC.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
